articles.title: MP Heatwave Red Alert: Temperatures Cross 46°C in 4 Districts - Dainik Jagran MP CG
articles.full_text:
MP Heatwave Red Alert: Temperatures Cross 46°C in 4 Districts
Digital Desk
IMD issues red alert for severe heatwave in 4 Madhya Pradesh districts. Temperatures are crossing 46°C. Advisory issued for next 10 days until May 31.
MP Heatwave Red Alert: 4 Districts Cross 46°C as Temperatures Soar
Severe heatwave conditions trigger red alert in Niwari, Chhatarpur, Panna, and Satna; IMD advises against outdoor exposure between noon and 3 PM.
Even before the formal start of the nine-day Nautapa period, large parts of Madhya Pradesh are reeling under an intense heat spell, forcing the India Meteorological Department (IMD) to issue a red alert for four districts on Friday.
Temperatures are already pushing past the 46-degree Celsius mark in Niwari, Chhatarpur, Panna, and Satna. Officials at the IMD Bhopam confirmed that the mercury is expected to remain above this threshold for the next 72 hours at least. The morning hours themselves are brutal—between 9 AM and 11 AM, the temperature is touching 38 to 40 degrees across several city centres.
Red Alert in Four Districts
The IMD has painted the northern and eastern belts of the state in deep red. Niwari, Chhatarpur, Panna, and Satna are under a red alert for severe heatwave conditions, with the maximum temperature likely to stay above 46 degrees. This is the highest category of warning, indicating a very high risk of heat illness for all age groups.
A broader orange alert has been issued for 21 districts, including Gwalior, Bhind, Morena, Sheopur, and Shivpuri. Meanwhile, a yellow alert is in place for 20 districts, including the state capital Bhopal, Ujjain, and Raisen, where temperatures are hovering between 43 and 45 degrees. Officials added that even in districts without a direct heatwave warning, like Indore and Dewas, humid conditions are making the heat unbearable.
IMD Advisory for Public
Weather scientist H.S. Pandey told reporters that the impact will be most severe between noon and 3 PM. "People should avoid stepping out unless absolutely necessary," he said. The department has issued a standard advisory: wear light-coloured cotton clothing, stay hydrated throughout the day, and pay special attention to children and the elderly.
Local authorities in the red-zone districts have been asked to ensure power supply for cooling centres and maintain water availability in public spaces.
Nine-Day Nautapa Spell Ahead
The situation is unlikely to ease anytime soon. According to the IMD forecast, this severe heat spell will remain at its peak for the next 10 days, until May 31. The Nautapa period, which begins on May 25, is traditionally the hottest phase of the summer in central India. This year, it is arriving on the back of an already scorching May.
Officials indicated that major cities like Bhopal, Indore, Gwalior, and Jabalpur will see no relief from the intense sun during this period.
Historical Trends in Key Cities
Looking at the data from the past decade, this isn't entirely unprecedented but remains dangerous. In Bhopal, the record for May still stands at 46.7 degrees Celsius from 2016. Interestingly, the city has also seen a trend of pre-monsoon showers in May—every year from 2014 to 2023 recorded some rainfall. No such relief is forecast this week.
In Gwalior, which historically acts as a heat furnace for the state, the mercury has crossed 47 degrees once and crossed 46 degrees three times in the last ten years. The all-time record for the city remains 48.3 degrees, recorded on May 30, 1947. Jabalpur and Ujjain are also recording temperatures 4-5 degrees above normal for this time of the season.
Looking Ahead
With no western disturbance in sight to break the pattern, the IMD has reiterated that the next 10 days will test the state's resilience. Officials are monitoring the situation hour by hour, but for now, the message from Bhopal's weather centre is clear: stay indoors during the afternoon, keep drinking water, and do not ignore signs of heat exhaustion.

articles.title: IMD issues orange and yellow alerts amid intense heatwave in Madhya Pradesh - Newsonair
articles.search_term: heatwave ("Agar Malwa" OR Alirajpur OR Anuppur OR Ashoknagar OR Balaghat OR Barwani OR Betul OR Bhind OR Bhopal OR Burhanpur OR Chhatarpur OR Chhindwara OR Damoh OR Datia OR Dewas OR Dhar OR Dindori OR Guna OR Gwalior OR Harda OR Hoshangabad OR Indore OR Jabalpur OR Jhabua OR Katni OR Khandwa OR Khargone OR Mandla OR Mandsaur OR Morena OR Narsinghpur OR Neemuch OR Panna OR Raisen OR Rajgarh OR Ratlam OR Rewa OR Sagar OR Satna OR Sehore OR Seoni OR Shahdol OR Shajapur OR Sheopur OR Shivpuri OR Sidhi OR Singrauli OR Tikamgarh OR Ujjain OR Umaria OR Vidisha) Madhya Pradesh
articles.full_text:
Madhya Pradesh is scorching under a severe heatwave, with orange and yellow alerts issued for several districts as the mercury touched 47 degrees Celsius in the state for the first time this season.
The intense heat and blazing sun have made daily life extremely difficult. The India Meteorological Department has issued an orange alert for severe heatwave conditions in districts including Datia, Bhind, Rewa, Satna, Panna, Sagar, Chhatarpur, Tikamgarh and Niwadi.
A yellow alert has also been issued for Bhopal, Indore, Ujjain, Gwalior, Jabalpur and several other districts. The heatwave is expected to continue for the next three to four days.
On Tuesday, Naugaon recorded 47 degrees Celsius, while Khajuraho registered 46.4 degrees Celsius. This is the first time this season that the temperature has reached 47 degrees Celsius in the state. In 22 cities across Madhya Pradesh, the maximum temperature crossed 44 degrees Celsius.
